project_title: PeakProtocol Phase 6: Calendar Supplement Visibility + Multi-Source Food Diary

fields.problem_statement: The Pack Owner tracks a complex supplement routine with multiple scheduling types (daily, every N days, weekly, specific days), but the calendar view provides no visibility into which supplements are due or taken on any given day. The only indicator is a generic green activity dot — there is no way to see at a glance whether the morning stack was completed without drilling into supplement-specific views. Separately, the food diary is constrained to a single data source (USDA FoodData Central API), which lacks coverage for prepared meals, restaurant food, international cuisine, branded products, and custom recipes. When a food cannot be found, there is no fallback — the user must abandon the entry or guess at macros. Both gaps create daily friction: the supplement blind spot risks missed doses, and the food source limitation makes consistent nutrition tracking unreliable.

users.goal: See supplement schedule compliance at a glance on the calendar and log all food intake accurately regardless of data source availability
users.context: Single-user personal health optimization PWA. Uses the app morning and evening daily. Manages 10+ supplements across 4 schedule types. Tracks diverse food intake including home-cooked meals, restaurant food, and branded items that may not exist in USDA database.

scope_in: Supplement dots on calendar day cells — each active supplement scheduled for that day renders as a small colored dot on the calendar grid cell, with color coding to distinguish taken (green), skipped (amber), and pending/not-yet-logged (gray)

Calendar day detail supplement checklist — tapping a day opens a supplement checklist showing each scheduled supplement as a checkbox with name, dose, and time-of-day; checking a box logs it as taken with current timestamp; unchecking reverts to pending
Supplement color assignment — each supplement gets a persistent color from a predefined palette, stored in the supplements table, so dots are visually distinguishable across the calendar

Monthly supplement compliance heatmap — calendar cells with all supplements taken show a subtle green background tint; partial completion shows amber; nothing logged shows no tint — providing instant monthly compliance visibility
Batch supplement check-in — a 'Mark All Taken' button on the day detail for mornings when the entire stack was taken together, with individual override capability
Multi-source food search — expand food lookup beyond USDA to include OpenFoodFacts API for branded/packaged products, with USDA remaining the primary source and OpenFoodFacts as fallback

Manual food entry with AI macro estimation — when no API source has the food, user can type a free-text food description (e.g., 'homemade chicken stir fry with rice, large bowl') and request AI-powered calorie and macro estimation via a 'Calculate' button

Daily food list with deferred calculation — food items can be listed as text entries throughout the day without macro data; a 'Calculate All' button triggers batch lookup/estimation for all unresolved items at once, so logging is fast and calculation is on-demand
Food source indicator — each food entry displays a small badge showing its data source (USDA, OpenFoodFacts, AI estimate, Manual) so the user knows the confidence level of the macro data
Manual macro override — regardless of source, the user can tap any food entry to manually edit calories, protein, carbs, fat, and fiber values
Improved Quick-Add for foods — extend the existing Quick-Add UI to support typing a food name and getting results from all available sources (USDA + OpenFoodFacts) in a unified search results list
scope_out: Meal planning or meal prep scheduling — v2 feature
Supplement interaction warnings or contraindication checks — requires medical database, v2
Barcode scanning for food products — requires native camera access, v2
Supplement purchase tracking or restock reminders — v2
Social or sharing features — single-user app
Recipe builder with ingredient-level macro calculation — v2
Photo-based food recognition — requires ML model, v2
Nutrition goal setting or macro target alerts — v2
Integration with fitness trackers or wearables — v2
OpenAI/Anthropic API key management UI — key will be configured as a Wrangler secret like existing API keys
constraints: Must deploy on existing Cloudflare stack: Workers (Hono backend), Pages (SolidJS frontend), D1 (database), KV (sessions), R2 (storage)
Frontend must use SolidJS + UnoCSS + Vite — no framework changes
Single-user app with fixed 'owner' user ID and passcode auth — no multi-tenant considerations
D1 has no ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN support in all contexts — new columns may require migration with table recreation or new tables
Cloudflare Workers have 128MB memory limit and 30s CPU time for standard plan — AI macro estimation must use external API call, not local inference
Existing supplement_logs table schema must be preserved for backward compatibility — new fields should be additive
OpenFoodFacts API is free but rate-limited — must implement caching similar to existing USDA food_cache pattern
AI macro estimation requires an LLM API key (e.g., Anthropic or OpenAI) configured as a Wrangler secret
Calendar component currently uses a single 'activeDates' Set<string> prop — supplement dots require richer per-day data
Existing food_entries table already has fdc_id column — needs a source column to distinguish data origins

success_criteria.criterion: Calendar day cells display colored dots for each supplement scheduled on that date
success_criteria.measurement: For a day with 3 scheduled supplements (1 taken, 1 skipped, 1 pending), the calendar cell renders 3 dots: 1 green, 1 amber, 1 gray

success_criteria.criterion: Tapping a calendar day shows a supplement checklist with checkboxes
success_criteria.measurement: Day detail view lists all supplements scheduled for selected date with checkbox, name, dose, and time-of-day; checking a box calls supplement log API and persists taken status

success_criteria.criterion: Batch 'Mark All Taken' logs all pending supplements for the day in a single action
success_criteria.measurement: Clicking 'Mark All Taken' on a day with 5 pending supplements results in 5 supplement_logs records with taken_at timestamps; all checkboxes show as checked

success_criteria.criterion: Food search returns results from both USDA and OpenFoodFacts APIs
success_criteria.measurement: Searching 'Coca-Cola' returns branded result from OpenFoodFacts; searching 'chicken breast' returns USDA result; results are labeled with source

success_criteria.criterion: Manual food entry with AI estimation returns calorie and macro breakdown
success_criteria.measurement: Entering 'large bowl of chicken fried rice' and clicking Calculate returns estimated calories, protein, carbs, fat, and fiber values with 'AI estimate' source badge

success_criteria.criterion: Food items can be listed without macros and batch-calculated later
success_criteria.measurement: User adds 3 food items as text-only entries (no macros); clicking 'Calculate All' populates macro data for all 3 items; daily totals update accordingly

success_criteria.criterion: Each food entry displays its data source
success_criteria.measurement: Food entries show a visible badge: 'USDA', 'OFF' (OpenFoodFacts), 'AI', or 'Manual' depending on how the macro data was obtained

success_criteria.criterion: Manual macro override works on any food entry regardless of original source
success_criteria.measurement: Tapping a food entry with USDA-sourced data opens an edit form; changing calories from 200 to 250 persists the change and updates daily totals; source badge changes to 'Manual'

success_criteria.criterion: Supplement color assignments persist across sessions
success_criteria.measurement: Supplement 'Vitamin D' is assigned color #F59E0B; after page reload, calendar dots for Vitamin D still render in #F59E0B

success_criteria.criterion: Calendar monthly compliance heatmap provides visual feedback
success_criteria.measurement: A day with all supplements taken shows green tint on cell background; a day with partial compliance shows amber tint; a day with no logs shows no tint

prior_art: PeakProtocol Phases 1-5: existing supplement CRUD (5 endpoints), scheduling engine (4 schedule types), supplement logging API, compliance calculator, Calendar component with activeDates, food logging via USDA with cache, food search/entry APIs, saved foods library, FoodLogger UI, Quick-Add UI
D1 schema: supplements, supplement_logs, food_cache, saved_foods, food_entries tables already exist with indexes
Daily Summary API (GET /api/daily-summary/:date) already joins supplement_logs with supplements table and aggregates food entries by meal
Calendar.tsx component currently renders a single green dot per day via activeDates Set<string> — needs enhancement for per-supplement colored dots
